title: Filing Your First Claim
desc: Step-by-step from DD-214 to decision letter -- everything you need to file right the first time

sections.heading: :shield: Before You Start: Gather Your Records
sections.body:
Your **DD-214 (Member 4 copy)** is the single most important document in your claim. Without it, nothing moves. It proves your service dates, duty stations, and character of discharge.

> [!important] If you don't have your DD-214, request it through the **National Personnel Records Center (NPRC)** at eVetRecs immediately. This can take weeks -- start NOW.

---

**Beyond the DD-214, gather:**
- **Service treatment records (STRs)** -- every sick call, every complaint, every diagnosis
- **Private medical records** related to your conditions
- **Personnel records** showing deployments, MOS assignments, and hazardous duty

> [!tip] Under `38 CFR 3.303(a)`, VA must consider "the places, types and circumstances of service." Your deployment orders, MOS records, and hazardous duty assignments help establish the environment that caused or worsened your conditions -- even if your STRs are thin.

> [!warning] VA has been known to lose records. **Make copies of EVERYTHING.** Keep your own complete file -- digital and physical. If VA loses it and you don't have a backup, you're starting from scratch.

sections.heading: :tools: Step 1: Create Your VA.gov Account
sections.body:
Your VA.gov account is mission control for your entire claim. Set it up right and you'll save yourself headaches down the road.

**Do this now:**
1. Go to **VA.gov** and create a **Login.gov** or **ID.me** account
2. Verify your **contact information** and **mailing address**
3. Set up **direct deposit** details (this is where your money goes)
4. Check whether VA already has your **service treatment records** on file

> [!check] Before you file anything, log in and view your VA medical records and any previously submitted documents. Know what VA already has -- and what's missing.

> [!warning] If your STRs are missing from VA's records, request them separately through NPRC. But **do NOT let missing STRs delay your filing** -- file your Intent to File (next step) immediately and gather records in parallel.

sections.heading: :flag: Step 2: File an Intent to File (VA Form 21-0966)
sections.body:
This is the **single most important step** most veterans skip -- and it costs them thousands.

> [!important] Under `38 CFR 3.155(b)`, filing an **Intent to File (ITF)** locks in your effective date -- the date from which VA will pay benefits if your claim is granted. You then have **one full year** to submit the complete application.

---

**Three ways to file an ITF:**

| Method | How | Notes |
|--------|-----|-------|
| **Online** | Start an application on VA.gov | System saves ITF automatically |
| **Paper** | Submit `VA Form 21-0966` | Mail or hand-deliver |
| **Phone** | Call **1-800-827-1000** | Rep must document it per `38 CFR 3.155(b)(1)(iii)` |

The ITF only needs to identify the **general benefit** you're seeking (compensation). It does **not** need to list specific conditions.

> [!check] File your Intent to File TODAY -- even if you're months away from submitting your full claim. Under `38 CFR 3.400`, your effective date is the later of the claim date or the date entitlement arose. **Every day you wait is money left on the table.**

> [!quote] A veteran who files an ITF on January 1st and submits their full claim on June 1st gets back pay to January 1st. A veteran who skips the ITF and files on June 1st loses five months of compensation -- potentially thousands of dollars.

sections.heading: :link: Step 3: Consider Getting a VSO or Representative
sections.body:
You don't have to do this alone -- and the help is **free**.

> [!tip] Veterans Service Organizations (VSOs) like the **DAV**, **VFW**, **American Legion**, and state-level organizations have trained claims agents who know the system inside out. They can access VA's internal systems to track your claim in real time.

---

**What a good VSO will do for you:**
- Review your service records and **identify conditions you may have missed**
- Ensure your paperwork is **complete and properly formatted**
- **Advocate on your behalf** throughout the process
- Track your claim through VA's internal systems

**How to appoint representation:**

| Form | Use For |
|------|---------|
| `VA Form 21-22` | Appoint a VSO |
| `VA Form 21-22a` | Appoint a private attorney or claims agent |

> [!check] You can change representatives at any time. Don't feel locked in -- if your VSO isn't responsive, switch. Your claim is too important for someone who won't return calls.

> [!warning] Not all VSOs are created equal. Ask other veterans in your area who they recommend. A bad VSO is worse than no VSO -- they can miss deadlines and give wrong advice.

sections.heading: :search: Step 4: Identify All Your Conditions
sections.body:
Here's the rule: **claim everything, let VA decide.** Do not self-eliminate. Your job is to put it on paper; VA's job is to adjudicate it.

> [!important] List every condition you believe is connected to your military service -- both obvious injuries and conditions that developed over time. If it started in service or got worse because of service, claim it.

---

**Commonly overlooked conditions:**
- **Tinnitus** (ringing in the ears) -- one of the most commonly rated disabilities
- **Sleep apnea** -- especially common after deployments
- **Migraines** -- often linked to TBI or stress
- **Mental health** (PTSD, anxiety, depression) -- no shame, huge impact on ratings
- **Flat feet** (pes planus) -- worsened by years of rucking
- **GERD/acid reflux** -- often secondary to medications or stress
- **Joint conditions** that worsened over years of service

> [!check] Think about **secondary conditions** -- disabilities caused or aggravated by an already service-connected condition. Under `38 CFR 3.310(a)`, disability "proximately due to or the result of a service-connected disease or injury shall be service connected." Under `3.310(b)`, even **aggravation** of a nonservice-connected condition by a service-connected one qualifies.

> [!quote] A veteran with service-connected PTSD develops sleep apnea and GERD from medication side effects. Both are potentially secondary claims under 38 CFR 3.310 -- adding significant combined rating on top of the PTSD.

sections.heading: :target: Step 5: Build Your Evidence Package
sections.body:
This is where claims are won or lost. For each condition, you need **three things** to establish service connection under `38 CFR 3.303`:

| Element | What It Proves | Key Evidence |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| **Current diagnosis** | You have the condition now | Medical records, doctor's letter |
| **In-service event** | Something happened during service | STRs, buddy statements, deployment records |
| **Nexus** | The two are connected | Medical opinion letter, IMO |

---

**Building each element:**

**In-service evidence:** Service treatment records showing complaints, treatment, or diagnosis are powerful. If your STRs are silent, **buddy statements** from fellow service members who witnessed your condition or the event fill that gap.

**The nexus opinion:** Your treating physician can write an opinion letter, or you can get an **Independent Medical Opinion (IMO)** from a specialist.

> [!important] The magic words are **"at least as likely as not"** -- meaning a 50% or greater probability. This is the legal standard. Make sure your doctor uses this exact phrase.

> [!tip] Under `38 CFR 3.303(d)`, service connection can be granted for **any disease diagnosed after discharge** when all the evidence establishes it was incurred in service. You don't need a diagnosis from while you were in -- you need a nexus connecting it back.

> [!warning] Don't rely on VA to build your case for you. The stronger your initial submission, the faster and more favorable the outcome.

sections.heading: :doc: Step 6: Submit VA Form 21-526EZ
sections.body:
This is the main event. File online at **VA.gov** using `VA Form 21-526EZ` (Application for Disability Compensation).

> [!check] List ALL conditions and upload EVERY piece of supporting evidence. Do not hold anything back for later.

---

**For each condition, include:**
- The **specific diagnosis** (not just symptoms -- "lumbar degenerative disc disease," not "back pain")
- **When you first noticed it** during service
- **What in-service event or exposure** caused it
- **How it affects your daily life and work** right now

> [!tip] Be specific and vivid in your descriptions. "I cannot sit at my desk for more than 30 minutes without severe lower back pain" is infinitely better than "my back hurts."

After submission, VA sends an acknowledgment letter and begins the **development phase**. Under `38 CFR 3.159(c)`, VA has a **duty to assist** you in obtaining evidence -- including service records, VA medical records, and scheduling C&P exams.

> [!warning] VA's duty to assist is real but limited. They'll request records and schedule exams, but they won't build your argument for you. The veterans who submit strong, complete packages up front get faster and better results.

sections.heading: :alert: Step 7: The C&P Exam
sections.body:
For most conditions, VA will schedule a **Compensation & Pension exam**. This is NOT a treatment appointment -- the examiner's job is to document severity and provide a nexus opinion.

> [!danger] Military culture teaches us to minimize pain and push through. The C&P exam is where that instinct costs you money. Describe your WORST days, not your best.

---

**Bring to every C&P exam:**
- List of **all medications** with dosages
- Your **supporting documents**
- Notes about **how your condition affects daily activities**
- Specific examples of **functional limitations**

> [!tip] If the examiner's report contains errors or doesn't reflect what you told them, you can submit a **rebuttal statement** or request a new exam. Under `38 CFR 3.159(c)(4)`, VA has a duty to provide an **adequate examination** -- one that considers the full record and provides a reasoned opinion.

> [!check] Read our **C&P Exam Survival Guide** for the complete breakdown on how to prepare, what examiners test, and what to do if it goes wrong.

sections.heading: :star: Step 8: Track, Respond, and Don't Give Up
sections.body:
The average processing time is **3-6 months**, but complex claims can take longer. Stay engaged.

> [!important] Monitor your claim status on **VA.gov** regularly. If VA sends a development letter requesting additional evidence, **respond promptly**. You typically have 30 days before VA may decide on what's in the file -- though you have up to one year under `38 CFR 3.159(b)(1)`.

---

**If your claim is denied or rated lower than expected, you have three appeal options:**

| Lane | Form | Best When |
|------|------|-----------|
| **Supplemental Claim** | `VA Form 20-0995` | You have **new evidence** to submit |
| **Higher-Level Review** | `VA Form 20-0996` | You believe VA made an **error of fact or law** |
| **Board Appeal** | `VA Form 10182` | You want a **Veterans Law Judge** to review |

> [!check] You have **one year** from the decision date to file in any lane while preserving your effective date. Don't let the clock run out.

> [!tip] A denial is NOT the end. Many successful claims were initially denied. The appeal system exists because VA gets it wrong -- a lot. Stay in the fight.

sections.heading: :fire: Common First-Claim Mistakes
sections.body:
These are the mistakes that cost veterans the most money and time. Avoid every single one.

> [!danger] **Not filing an Intent to File first** -- this is the #1 money-losing mistake. Every day without an ITF is a day of back pay you'll never recover.

---

**The full list of claim-killers:**

| Mistake | Why It Hurts |
|---------|--------------|
| Skipping the ITF | Lose months or years of back pay |
| Filing too few conditions | You left ratings (and money) on the table |
| Submitting without evidence | VA won't build your case for you |
| Missing the C&P exam | Almost always an automatic denial |
| Describing best-day symptoms | Examiner documents lower severity |
| No nexus letter with weak STRs | No link between service and condition |
| Ignoring secondary conditions | Often worth more than the primary under `38 CFR 3.310` |
| Accepting denial as final | You have appeal rights -- use them |

> [!check] The veterans who win are the ones who **prepare thoroughly**, **file strategically**, and **never stop fighting** for what they've earned.

> [!quote] "I almost didn't file for tinnitus because I thought ringing ears was normal after the military. That 10% rating pushed me from 60% to 70% combined -- unlocking dependent pay and an extra $400/month." -- V2V Community Member
